tier_1.name: Billing must be accurate
tier_1.basis: 0 bills held by the pre-bill validation gate (Phase 3)
tier_1.rationale: Director's Principle 1, binding: 100% of bills validated before issue, zero tolerance, continuous. High likelihood because every bill run hits the same code paths at volume -- a latent bug class (R10's C6 SME-as-Household precedent) recurs every run until the class itself is closed, not sampled.

tier_1.name: Back-billing cap -- cannot bill >12 months of unbilled consumption
tier_1.source: Ofgem SLC 31A / Citizens Advice back-billing guidance
tier_1.rationale: Real financial harm if breached (surprise multi-year bill), but the forced catch-up mechanism in simulation/meter_reads.py already structurally prevents the cap being exceeded -- medium not high likelihood.

tier_1.name: Credit refunds within 10 working days of account closure
tier_1.rationale: Real customer financial harm (delayed money) if the SLA is missed.

tier_1.name: Guaranteed Standards of Performance -- automatic compensation payments
tier_1.basis: Tracked by company/billing (GSOP compensation physics, Phase 3 unhappy-paths)
tier_1.rationale: Missed auto-compensation is direct customer financial harm plus a licence breach.

tier_1.name: Priority Services Register -- vulnerable customer welfare duties
tier_1.source: Ofgem SLC 0 / Consumer Duty / PSR Code of Practice
tier_1.rationale: The one obligation in this register where a failure (missing a PSR flag before a supply interruption for a life-support/medically-dependent customer) can plausibly cause physical harm, not just financial loss -- Tier 1 regardless of likelihood, per the director's impact ranking. Likelihood is genuinely low in this sim (no real supply interruptions modelled yet) but the tier does not relax because of that -- impact alone forces Tier 1 here.

tier_1.name: VAT must be charged at the correct rate by customer segment
tier_1.source: VAT Act 1994 (domestic 5%, business 20%, de minimis rules)
tier_1.basis: 0 bills held by the pre-bill validation gate (Phase 3)
tier_1.rationale: Same class as R10's C6 defect (SME billed as Household at 20% VAT instead of 5%) -- confirmed high-likelihood given it already happened. Tier 1, needs the invariants library (Phase 2) to give the gate (Phase 3) a real segment->rate map.

tier_3.name: Settlement / industry-code duties (BSC, MRA metering data obligations)
tier_3.rationale: Licence/regulatory impact, low likelihood given settlement already anchored to real Elexon data.

tier_3.name: Marketing and switching conduct rules (cooling-off, misleading claims)
tier_3.source: Consumer Contracts Regulations 2013 / Ofgem SLC (marketing standards)
tier_3.rationale: Licence/regulatory impact; the 14-day cooling-off constant is already hard-anchored.

tier_3.name: Smart meter rollout progress reporting
tier_3.rationale: Reporting obligation, not a customer-facing control; existing tracker already RAGs this.

tier_3.name: Warm Home Discount / ECO social obligation spend
tier_3.rationale: Mandatory spend tracked continuously by an existing dedicated register.

tier_3.name: Green tariff REGO backing must match claimed renewable supply
tier_3.rationale: Mis-selling risk is real but reputational/licence, not direct customer financial harm; existing tracker already audits this.

tier_3.name: Consumer Duty -- good outcomes across products, price, understanding, support
tier_3.source: FCA-style Consumer Duty applied via Ofgem SLC 0 (2023)
tier_3.rationale: Broad conduct standard; existing scorecard already RAGs the four pillars periodically.

consumer_duty.narrative: Loss-framed retention uplift over gain-framed is 0.0pp higher for residential customers than the average of other segments. No material concentration detected.

insights.executive_summary: Business survived the full 2016-2025 window including the 2021-22 crisis. Net margin £1,526,708 (8.1% of revenue -- above 2-5% benchmark). Hedging cost £4,223,875 vs going naked (85% mandate price). 489 bill shock events (118 in crisis years); 12/12 retention offers accepted.

insights.headline: Hedging cost £4,223,875 vs going naked (85% mandate price)
insights.narrative: Whole-run commodity basis: actual net £1,436,697 vs naked £5,660,572. The 85% minimum hedge mandate cost £4,223,875 over the 10-year window. Worst year: 2022 (hedging cost £1,022,784 vs naked). Hedging reduces variance (survival objective) at the cost of expected P&L.

insights.headline: 489 bill shock events (118 in crisis years); 12/12 retention offers accepted
insights.narrative: Bill shocks across 2016-2025: 489 events, 11 extreme (>100%). 2021-22 accounted for 118 shocks as wholesale prices spiked. Retention: 12/12 offers accepted (100%); 6 customers churned without an offer (margin guard blocked). Enterprise value: £8,160,654 across 15 accounts.

insights.headline: Risk committee: 37 interventions; SURVIVED full 2016-2025 window
insights.narrative: The risk committee (Context Handshake) intervened 37 times over 10 years, with 8 in the 2021-22 crisis. Business survived: No administration event -- Ofgem solvency floor maintained.. The 85% minimum hedge mandate limited worst-case exposure.

insights.headline: Net margin £1,526,708 (8.1% of revenue -- above 2-5% benchmark)
insights.narrative: Gross margin £6,433,656, net £1,526,708 (8.1% of revenue). Industry benchmark: 2-5% -- this business sits above that range. I&C customers contribute 99% of net margin (£1,515,629); residential 1% (£11,078). WARNING: I&C accounts for 99% of margin -- high concentration risk. Cost to serve: £18,727; net after CTS: £6,414,930.

insights.headline: 1550 bills issued; service quality 0.903/1.0, clarity 0.812/1.0
insights.narrative: 1550 bills issued over 2016-2025 (avg 155/yr). Service quality: 0.903 (excellent). Bill clarity: 0.812 (good). Cost to serve: £18,727 portfolio total, £1,248/account/run.

insights.coherence_narrative: The dominant theme is crisis survival. The business survived the full 2016-2025 window including the 2021-22 energy crisis, with the 85% hedge mandate cost £4,223,875 vs going naked, 118 bill shocks in the 2021-22 crisis, and 12/12 retention offers accepted. net margin £1,526,708 (8.1% of revenue -- above 2-5% benchmark), with I&C customers contributing 99% of net margin. The hedging mandate limited worst-case downside at the cost of expected P&L in calmer years; the I&C book was the primary profit engine throughout. Without I&C revenue the residential book would be loss-making -- concentration risk is the key strategic vulnerability.
recommended_actions: I&C concentration critical (99% of margin): target acquisition of 2 additional I&C customers to reduce dependency
Hedge mandate cost £4,223,875 vs going naked: review minimum hedge fraction for non-crisis years (current 85% floor)
118 crisis-year bill shocks: consider tariff smoothing mechanism to spread 2021-22 wholesale spike across 2020-2023 for residential customers

frozen_baseline.narrative: Replaying the same decade under the naive pre-learning policy (flat 5% retention discount, margin-only offer guard, no VaR-constrained hedge decision -- the pre-Phase-14a/15b/43b state) instead of today's decisions changes enterprise value by £159,745 (14 offers made under current policy vs 16 under naive, 14 vs 16 retained).
